caption: Black Hills Olowan. Style: orchestral, Native American, Lakota. Commissioned by the SDSO in 2009. Honors the relationship between the Black Hills and the Lakota people. Integrates the traditional Lakota song 'Heart Butte Special' into an orchestral texture. In the concluding moments, the singers become the leading voice.

Wind on Clear Lake. Style: orchestral, Native American, Dakota flute. Composed for the SDSO and Dakota flutist Bryan Akipa. Describes Clear Lake in South Dakota, its deep spirituality. Soundscape suggests waves lapping, leaves rustling, wind chimes, distant thunder, frogs, barn swallows.

Victory Songs. Style: orchestral, Lakota language, song cycle. Orchestral rhapsody sung in the Lakota language, inspired by writings of Charles Alexander Eastman, composed in honor of Lloyd Running Bear Sr. and all Lakota Indian warriors. Each movement depicts a legendary Lakota warrior and his deeds.

Desert Wind. Style: chamber, Native American, guitar. Originally composed on a pawn shop electric guitar. Described as both contemplative and a primal cry into the beautiful and endless open spaces of the natural world.

Amazing Grace. Style: orchestral, traditional, Lakota, arrangement. Inspired by the discovery that Amazing Grace is a traditional song for Lakota drumming groups. Created while composer-in-residence at SDSO. Wiprud writes: 'Perhaps this work captures what we're doing here — finding common ground as equals between two long musical traditions.'
Into the Sunset. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
When I Close My Eyes. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
When I Last Seen You. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Such Good Memories. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song

track_title: Can't Get You Off My Mind
caption: Can't Get You Off My Mind. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Baby Girl. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
In the Morning. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Your Pretty Smile. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Ten Years. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Thank-You. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song

track_title: As Long as You Come Home Tonite
caption: As Long as You Come Home Tonite. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song

track_title: Please, Don't Be Mad at Me
caption: Please, Don't Be Mad at Me. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Wedding Song (My Promise). Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Good Bye!. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song
Forever. Style: contemporary, Indigenous, original song

track_title: Lakota National Anthem (Flag Song) and Veterans' Song
caption: Lakota National Anthem (Flag Song) and Veterans' Song. Style: traditional, Lakota, flag song, veterans. Flag Song — The President's Flag will stand forever. Veterans' Song — often follows the Flag Song at the raising or lowering of the U.S. flag.
Little Big Horn Battle Song. Style: traditional, Lakota, battle song, historical. A song made for the Battle of Little Big Horn (1876).

track_title: Our Friends Came With The Soldiers
caption: Our Friends Came With The Soldiers. Style: traditional, Lakota, historical, conflict. A song made for the Eastern Lakota who scouted for the U.S. cavalry against the Western Lakota.

Sneak-Up Dance. Style: traditional, Lakota, dance, warrior. A Sneak-Up Dance for a warrior who was wounded in battle. One of the warriors was knocked off his horse and the others picked him up and are carrying him back.
World War I Veterans' Song. Style: traditional, Lakota, veterans, WWI. This song exemplifies the patriotism of the Lakota.
The Jealous Woman. Style: traditional, Lakota, social, humor. One of the Porcupine Singers' standard songs.
The Bark of the Dog. Style: traditional, Lakota, raiding, ceremonial. The leader of a successful raiding party barks like a dog four times in advance of the returning party.
Song of the Fallen Warrior. Style: traditional, Lakota, memorial, warrior. A memorial song made for One Feather.

Birthday Song. Style: traditional, Lakota, birthday, ceremonial. A song of encouragement sung for a person having a birthday. The Lakota name of the person being honored is inserted at the beginning of the song.

Song of the Dancers. Style: traditional, Lakota, dance, identity. This song expresses the sentiment of many dancers caught between their desire to follow the traditional ways and the pressures of the modern world.

Hiawatha Melodrama (after Dvořák): I. Prologue. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. World Premiere Recording. Version III ('final') of the Hiawatha Melodrama, premiered by PostClassical Ensemble, Angel Gil-Ordonez, and Kevin Deas at Clarice Smith Performing Arts Center, 1 March 2013. Text from Longfellow's The Song of Hiawatha (Prologue and Chapters 10, 11, 12, 17, 20, 22).

Hiawatha Melodrama: II. Hiawatha's Wooing. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. Music adapts the Larghetto from Dvorak's Violin Sonatina (composed in Iowa, 1893) and portions of the Largo from the New World Symphony. The Larghetto (as Beckerman documented) is a portrait of Minnehaha ('Laughing Water'), Hiawatha's wife - Dvorak alternates major/minor modes to depict her moods.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: III. Hiawatha's Wedding Feast
caption: Hiawatha Melodrama: III. Hiawatha's Wedding Feast. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. Music extracted from the Scherzo of the New World Symphony, which Dvorak testified begins with stomping, whirling music inspired by the Dance of Pau-Puk-Keewis at Hiawatha's wedding. The alignments with the song of Chibiabos and the tale told by Iagoo are Beckerman's scholarship.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: IV. The Death of Minnehaha
caption: Hiawatha Melodrama: IV. The Death of Minnehaha. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. Music begins with a C-sharp minor passage from American Suite movement III in Dvorak's 'Indian' style, continuing with a C-sharp minor passage from the New World Symphony Largo. Czech writer Katerina Emingerova singled out the forest funeral as a key source passage for the Largo.

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: V. The Hunting of Pau-Puk-Keewis
caption: Hiawatha Melodrama: V. The Hunting of Pau-Puk-Keewis. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. Music extracted from the New World Symphony finale (a Beckerman alignment). Certain Longfellow lines are here sung. Beckerman: 'As soon as Dvorak returned to Bohemia in 1895, he composed tone poems based on ballads of K.J. Erben, setting down the poem, line by line, beneath the music.'

track_title: Hiawatha Melodrama: VI. Epilogue — Hiawatha's Departure
caption: Hiawatha Melodrama: VI. Epilogue — Hiawatha's Departure. Style: orchestral, melodrama, American, Longfellow, Dvořák. Music recalls the American Suite theme from Part Three and passages from the New World Symphony. Horowitz: the singular ending - dirge, apotheosis, diminuendo - can only be programmatic.

track_title: Goin' Home (after Dvořák Symphony No. 9 'From the New World': II. Largo)
caption: Goin' Home (after Dvořák Symphony No. 9 'From the New World': II. Largo). Style: orchestral, song, American, Dvořák, spirituals. Text by William Arms Fisher (1861-1948), a student of Dvorak at the National Conservatory, who adapted the famous Largo melody in 1922. The song became associated in the U.S.

track_title: Violin Sonatina in G major Op. 100 B. 183: II. Larghetto (1893)
genre_tags: chamber; violin; piano; Czech; American period
caption: Violin Sonatina in G major Op. 100 B. 183: II. Larghetto (1893). Style: chamber, violin, piano, Czech, American period. Previously released on Naxos 8.554413 (c) 2001. Composed in Spillville, Iowa, 1893. Popularized by Fritz Kreisler as the Indian Lament; Beckerman documented it as a portrait of Minnehaha. Zhou Qian plays a 1757 J.B. Guadagnini, generously loaned by Mr. and Mrs. Rin Kei Mei.

track_title: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 4. Poco andante in F major (1894)
caption: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 4. Poco andante in F major (1894). Style: piano, Czech, Humoresque. Composed in Prague between Dvorak's stints in Manhattan; American and Bohemian styles juxtaposed. Fourth Humoresque opens with what sounds like a foreshadowing of Gershwin's Porgy and Bess (composed four decades later).

track_title: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 7. Poco lento e grazioso in G flat major (1894)
caption: Eight Humoresques Op. 101 B. 187: No. 7. Poco lento e grazioso in G flat major (1894). Style: piano, Czech, Humoresque. The Seventh Humoresque hums a dance tune so familiar in the U.S. that many Americans assume an American composed it. Dvorak's American and Bohemian styles are interwoven throughout the Humoresques.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': I. Moderato (1894–95)
caption: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': I. Moderato (1894–95). Style: piano, Czech, American period, suite. Begun in New York just after the New World Symphony premiere; scored for solo piano then orchestrated 1895. In Spillville, Iowa, Dvorak listened to interracial Kickapoo Medicine Show musicians intermingling Native American dances with banjo and guitar.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': II. Molto vivace
caption: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': II. Molto vivace. Style: piano, Czech, American period, suite. Movement II (Molto vivace). The American Suite's defining quality is simplicity - serene speech, shunning compositional virtuosity.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': III. Allegretto
caption: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': III. Allegretto. Style: piano, Czech, American period, suite. Movement III (Allegretto). A jaunty dance not far removed from the world of stride piano. The elegiac Indianist refrain from this movement (C-sharp minor) is extracted for the Hiawatha Melodrama's Part Three (The Death of Minnehaha).

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': IV. Andante
caption: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': IV. Andante. Style: piano, Czech, American period, suite. Movement IV (Andante). Evokes the vacant Iowa landscape Dvorak found 'sometimes very sad, sad to despair.' Prairie vacancy mates with cakewalk in the American Suite.

track_title: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': V. Allegro
caption: Suite in A major Op. 98 B. 184 'American': V. Allegro. Style: piano, Czech, American period, suite. Movement V (Allegro). An A major 'Indian' tune turns into an A major minstrel song - fusing the Indianist and African-American musical traditions Dvorak encountered during his American residency.

Navajo War Dance No. 2 Op. 29 (1904). Style: piano, American, Indianist, Navajo. Navajo War Dance No. 2 (1904), dedicated to John Kirkpatrick (later to champion Ives' Concord Sonata). With dissonance and rhythmic bite (remarkably progressive for 1904), it suggests a New World Bartok.

track_title: Mesa and Plain Op. 20 No. 2: Pawnee Horses (1905)
caption: Mesa and Plain Op. 20 No. 2: Pawnee Horses (1905). Style: piano, American, Indianist, Pawnee, Omaha. Based on an Omaha song Farwell considered so rhythmically complex it could not be performed by 'any known singer except an Indian.' Piano version (Op. 20), barely a minute long.

genre_tags: choral; American; Indianist; Pawnee; a cappella
caption: Pawnee Horses (version for chorus) Op. 102 (1937). Style: choral, American, Indianist, Pawnee, a cappella. World Premiere Recording. In 1937, Farwell created this eight-part a cappella choral version for John Finlay Williamson's Westminster Choir - described as 'an American choral masterpiece.' Based on the same Omaha source as Op. 20 No. 2, expanded into a full choral work.
